report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Jordan, thanks for sharing all of this with us. Your answers paint a really coherent picture, and we think it will be encouraging to see how connected these patterns actually are.

### Your Weight Loss Efforts May Be Working Against You

This is probably the most important thing to understand: the calorie counting, intermittent fasting, and intense exercise you've tried are all well-intentioned, but they share a common metabolic consequence. [Caloric restriction consistently lowers circulating T3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2649744/), your body's most active thyroid hormone and the master regulator of metabolic rate. [Fasting further amplifies cortisol secretion](https://mayoclinic.elsevierpure.com/en/publications/fasting-as-a-metabolic-stress-paradigm-selectively-amplifies-cort), your primary stress hormone, which promotes fat storage (especially in the face and midsection) and water retention. Each cycle of restriction may have slowed your metabolism a little more, making weight loss progressively harder. Your body isn't resisting you out of spite. It's conserving energy because it's been told, repeatedly, that fuel is scarce.

### The Cold Hands Tell a Bigger Story

You mentioned frequently cold extremities, which is one of the most reliable indicators of a suppressed metabolic rate, specifically low thyroid function. When thyroid output drops, the body reduces heat production and circulation to the periphery. Pair this with your stuck weight, puffiness, and the fatigue you describe ("used to have so much energy"), and a picture of metabolic slowdown becomes hard to ignore. Your energy rating of 7/10 suggests you've adapted to a lower baseline, but "feeling like yourself again" likely means your body once operated at a much higher level.

### Low Appetite as a Stress Signal

Having to force yourself to eat might seem unrelated to weight struggles, but it's actually a key piece of the puzzle. [Appetite suppression is a hallmark of chronic HPA axis activation](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10077914/), even when you don't feel emotionally stressed. Your body can be running on stress hormones (cortisol, adrenaline) to compensate for insufficient fuel, which suppresses hunger while simultaneously making fat loss nearly impossible. You reported low stress but poor sleep, and that combination is telling: the stress may be metabolic rather than psychological, driven by undereating, blood sugar drops, and the fasting patterns you've relied on.

### Poor Sleep and the Nighttime Energy Gap

Poor sleep in the context of low appetite and a history of caloric restriction often points to nighttime blood sugar instability. When the body doesn't have enough stored fuel (particularly liver glycogen from carbohydrates), stress hormones rise overnight to mobilize energy. This can fragment sleep, reduce sleep depth, and leave you feeling unrested. Your current diet, while not extreme, leans heavily on grains and plant-based carbs (oatmeal, sandwich bread, pasta) that may not be optimally supporting liver glycogen stores or providing the nutrient density your metabolism needs right now.

### Digestion Deserves Attention

You noted digestive issues, and given everything else, this fits. Thyroid hormones drive stomach acid production, enzyme secretion, and gut motility. When metabolic rate drops, digestion slows, and the environment becomes more favorable for bacterial imbalances. Poor digestion also means you're extracting less nutrition from the food you do eat, which compounds the energy deficit. Your diet is also relatively high in the types of fibers and starches that feed bacterial fermentation, which can worsen bloating, gas, and other GI symptoms.

---

This is a lot of interconnection from a short quiz, and that's actually the point. Your symptoms aren't a random collection of unrelated problems. They suggest a coherent pattern centered around metabolic rate and energy production. A deeper look at your full history, labs, and lifestyle would help us understand exactly where the leverage points are, but the patterns here are clear and, importantly, addressable.
